problem_summary: 2019 MCM Problem C (data): characterize the spatial-temporal spread of the opioid crisis across five Appalachian states (KY, OH, PA, VA, WV) using county-level NFLIS illicit-drug-report data (2010-2016) plus ACS socioeconomic data; analyze the association of socioeconomic factors with opioid abuse; and recommend government policy. Team 1901679 (control #1901679) won Outstanding with a deliberate dual-model strategy: a from-scratch stochastic random-walk simulation and a suite of spatial regression models, cross-compared at the end.
models_used: Drug taxonomy preprocessing: collapsed 69 NFLIS opioid substances into 4 chemistry/availability categories (mild painkillers/agonists like methadone-buprenorphine, semi-synthetics like oxycodone-hydrocodone, synthetics+heroin like fentanyl/U-48800, natural alkaloids like morphine-codeine) to reveal trends despite cross-lab labeling noise

Discrete-class state scheme: each county labeled into 6 classes (Low/High x Stable/Increasing/Decreasing) via data-driven cutoffs — median for prevalence, +1 SD (increasing) and -0.5 SD (decreasing, asymmetric on purpose) for rate-of-change, read off histograms; an earlier 4-class version kept for sensitivity analysis

MODEL I — custom time-inhomogeneous stochastic random walk / DTDS Markov agent model: each county is an agent; next-state probability vector built from (a) uniform noise per class (optimized, best=0.3), (b) neighbor 'votes' with inverse-distance weighting (adjacent=1, one county away=1/4), (c) division by a hand-designed 6x6 penalty/transition-difficulty matrix, (d) multiplication by optimized per-class coefficient vector, then normalized; Monte-Carlo over many trials with 3-month time steps, majority vote per county. Combines SIR/SIS compartmental thinking, Greenwood model (Markov+binomial SIR), Hagerstrand Monte-Carlo diffusion, and agent-based modeling

Custom error/objective function inspired by Moran's I global spatial autocorrelation: A_class = mean over counties of (#neighbors with same class / #neighbors); Err = sum over classes of |A_predicted - A_real|. Used to optimize noise level and class coefficients — chosen specifically because a 'good' result is matching the spatial clustering pattern, not exact per-county labels

Random Forest classifier (sklearn, 90% test accuracy, beat logistic regression) over 23 socioeconomic predictors to produce class probabilities that initialize the random walk's probability vector; sklearn feature_importances_ used to rank socioeconomic drivers (top = total illicit drug-use rate, total population, US-born, ancestry, English-only, education)

Monte-Carlo origin back-simulation: ran the no-SES random walk forward from each county as a seed starting in year 2000, propagated to 2010, compared to real 2010 data; lowest-dissimilarity seeds = likely origins (most likely Montgomery County, KY)

MODEL II — Spatial regression suite (R spdep): Spatial Lag/SAR (lagsarlm, y=ρWy+Xβ+u, spillover/diffusion), Spatial Error/SEM (errorsarlm, spatially correlated unobserved risk factors), and Spatial Durbin (lagsarlm type='mixed', y=ρWy+Xβ+WXθ+e, combines both). Run separately per year (2010-2016) since the package allows one value per region; aggregated to study temporal non-stationarity
Spatial weights W from a US-county shapefile subset to the 5 states, converted to a neighbor/weights object (adjacency-based)

impacts() decomposition into Direct (local), Indirect (spillover), and Total effects of each predictor — needed because spatial-lag coefficients are not directly interpretable; headline result: a unit increase in total illicit-drug-use rate raises opioid abuse rate ~52% (avg total impact 0.517 vs 0.374 from ordinary OLS)

Forecasting 2017-2021 via predict.sarlm with type='trend' on linearly-extrapolated SES variables, then re-binned through the 6-class classifier to make spatial-regression output directly comparable to the random-walk output
key_techniques: Variance Inflation Factor (VIF=1/(1-R_k^2)) feature screening: pruned 149 ACS variables to 54 by dropping VIF>10 multicollinear predictors, with a deliberate, justified exception to keep education variables (VIF ~8-9) for domain reasons
Designing a bespoke objective function matched to the model's true success criterion (spatial autocorrelation match) rather than reusing an off-the-shelf accuracy metric
Penalty/transition matrix encoding domain priors (e.g. harder to go High-Stable -> Low-Stable than to relapse) directly into the stochastic kernel
Asymmetric statistical cutoffs (+1 SD vs -0.5 SD) justified by the real-world claim that counties slip into abuse more easily than they recover, and that the data's 'normal' is already crisis-era

Formal model selection and significance testing: Log-Likelihood comparison (Durbin always best), Likelihood Ratio Test (spatial vs OLS, p<=2^-3), AIC, Lagrange Multiplier test for residual autocorrelation, Breusch-Pagan test for heteroskedasticity

Proxy-variable strategy with explicit assumptions when desired variables (income, race, healthcare, disability) were absent: education as proxy for income/healthcare, ancestry+language as proxy for race, veteran status as proxy for disability

Two independent models built to cover each other's blind spots, then quantitatively cross-validated (deviation-error curve between the two forecasts), turning model disagreement into a tool for stating WHICH model to trust over WHICH horizon
Numerical-stability engineering to get spatial regressions to converge: rescaling large-magnitude predictors, adjusting numerical tolerance, removing correlated variables
Validating model output against external CDC statistics (e.g. 61% of 2014 overdose deaths involved opioids ~ the 52% impact estimate) for face validity
why_it_won: "From a judge's perspective this paper wins on intellectual honesty and methodological depth applied to a DATA problem. (1) The dual-model approach is not redundant — each model answers what the other cannot (random walk captures spatial diffusion dynamics but is insensitive to SES; spatial regression quantifies SES impacts and gives interpretable, significance-tested coefficients), and the team explicitly reasons about when to trust each. (2) Every modeling choice is justified with either domain literature or a statistical test — nothing is a black box: VIF for feature selection, LRT/AIC/LogLik for model selection, BP/LM tests for diagnostics. (3) The team invented its own machinery where the problem demanded it (the Moran's-I-inspired error function, the penalty matrix, the discrete 6-class scheme) rather than forcing a textbook model, which signals genuine modeling maturity. (4) It directly answers the actual policy question with a concrete, defensible lever (reduce TOTAL illicit drug use, not just opioids, backed by the 'gateway' literature and the dominant impact coefficient). (5) Outstanding-level self-criticism: it names the Ecological Fallacy, the Modifiable Areal Unit Problem, adjacency-vs-distance weighting, the semi-Markov violation, and the danger of linear SES extrapolation — pre-empting every objection a judge would raise. (6) Strong narrative cohesion ('Random Walks and Rehab', the recurring Brownlea ring-effect motif) ties contagion science to the math."
section_highlights: "Summary sheet is exemplary: states both models, the headline 52% finding, the cross-model agreement, AND the validity windows (spatial regression best 0-4 yrs, random walk best 4-7 yrs) — a judge knows the whole story in one page. Strong Background/Etiology section grounds the math in real addiction science and a wave-by-wave epidemic timeline, earning credibility before any equation. The 'Overarching Concepts' section (Ecological Fallacy, spillover-vs-correlated-risk-factors framing) sets up the entire spatial-regression rationale and is reused as the model-selection logic. Each model has its own Part with How-it-Works steps, Evaluation, Testing, and Sensitivity Analysis subsections — disciplined parallel structure. The two-model comparison section with the deviation-error chart is the analytical climax. A dedicated multi-test Statistical Results section (LogLik table per year, impacts table per year) shows rigor. Honest, granular Limitations section split Overall / Model I / Model II. A genuinely actionable Memo to the (named, real) DEA Acting Administrator with 5 concrete prioritized policies. Heavy, properly-cited literature use (63 references) for a data problem."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Linear extrapolation of all SES variables for 2017-2021 forecasts is the team's own-admitted weakest link — error grows fast after 4-5 years, and all forecasts inherit bias from applying only the 2016 spatial-lag model forward

Spatial regressions had to be run one year at a time (spdep one-value-per-region limit) instead of a proper spatial panel model, so temporal information is only aggregated post hoc, not modeled jointly

Semi-Markov violation: next state depends on neighbors' current states which themselves depend on the past, so the nominal Markov property is not truly satisfied; long-horizon forecasts ignore policy/path-dependence and so are weak

Spatial-lag impact coefficients for SES variables (ancestry, language) are tiny and policy-uninformative — the model is essentially dominated by one predictor (total drug-use rate), so the rich socioeconomic analysis yields little actionable beyond 'reduce drugs overall'
Adjacency-based spatial weighting treats all counties as equally spaced regardless of size/distance; distance-based weighting could change results (acknowledged)
Asymptotic validity of spatial regressions for ~460 counties was assumed, not confirmed via the proper Monte-Carlo hypothesis tests (admitted)

Significant unresolved residual autocorrelation (LM tests) and heteroskedasticity (all BP tests significant) remain in the final Durbin models — known omitted spatially-correlated variables and population-size effects

Random-walk error is actually HIGH for the first ~6 simulated years before the cluster-vs-noise tension stabilizes, undermining its near-term forecasts (which is why they lean on the regression there)
Origin back-simulation initialized all counties to 'low stable' in 2000 and allows unrealistic single-step jumps to high classes, adding error to the Montgomery, KY origin claim

Markdown table renderings of the choropleth maps in this corpus copy are clearly OCR-garbled (e.g. wrong state names, runaway 'Maricopa' rows) — the figures themselves are unreliable in text form, though that's a digitization artifact, not a paper flaw
reusable_patterns: Dual-model 'complementary blind spots' pattern: pair a mechanistic/simulation model with a statistical/regression model, then quantify their forecast divergence to declare which model owns which time horizon — a powerful, judge-pleasing structure for any data or dynamics problem
Collapse a high-cardinality categorical column (69 drugs, 149 ACS variables) into a small number of domain-motivated buckets before modeling, to escape label noise and gain statistical power

Build a CUSTOM error/objective function that matches your model's real success criterion rather than defaulting to accuracy/MSE — here, a Moran's-I-style spatial-autocorrelation match — and use it to drive parameter optimization
VIF-based multicollinearity pruning (drop VIF>10) as a clean, citable feature-selection step for wide socioeconomic datasets, with documented exceptions for domain-critical variables
Encode domain priors as an explicit penalty/transition matrix inside a stochastic kernel to bias a simulation toward realistic behavior
When required variables are missing, declare explicit proxy assumptions (education->income, ancestry/language->race) up front in an Assumptions section and revisit them in Limitations

Use formal model-selection and diagnostic tests (Log-Likelihood, LRT, AIC, Lagrange Multiplier, Breusch-Pagan) to JUSTIFY model choice instead of asserting it — and state when a test cannot be used (e.g. LRT can't compare non-nested SAR vs SEM)
Decompose spatial/network model effects into Direct + Indirect + Total impacts so coefficients become interpretable and policy-relevant
Re-bin a continuous regression forecast through the same discrete classifier used by the simulation model so two heterogeneous models become directly comparable
Anchor quantitative findings to external authoritative statistics (CDC) for face validity, while respecting the 'no external data' rule by using them only for validation
Validity-window honesty in the summary: tell the reader the horizon over which each model is trustworthy — this builds enormous judge trust
Per-model parallel sub-structure (How it Works / Evaluation / Testing / Sensitivity / Limitations) keeps a multi-model paper navigable
Run the analysis on a reduced hand-picked variable set AND the full set, report that conclusions are stable, as a built-in sensitivity check
